summary: Ronacher argues that durable execution doesn’t require heavyweight infrastructure or new services; Postgres alone is enough for many real-world needs. He frames durable workflows as a queue plus a state store, which Postgres already provides via SKIP LOCKED and persistent storage. He introduces Absurd, a minimal SQL-only library that pushes workflow complexity into the database while keeping SDKs thin and ergonomic. The post shows how agent-style loops map cleanly onto this model through repeatable steps with checkpointing. He concludes that many teams overcomplicate durability and that a simpler, self-hostable approach is often more appropriate.
key_points: Durable execution is fundamentally a queue plus state storage, both already handled well by Postgres.
Absurd implements durable workflows with a single SQL file and thin SDKs, avoiding extra services or plugins.
Checkpointed steps prevent repeated work; failed tasks replay prior state without re-running steps.
Agent loops fit naturally by reusing a single step name with automatic checkpoint indexing.
Events and sleeps enable long-lived flows without needing an external orchestrator.
money_quotes: Durable execution can be thought of as the combination of a queue system and a state store that remembers the most recently seen execution state.
Absurd at the core is a single `.sql` file ... which needs to be applied to a database of your choice.
Tasks are subdivided into *steps*, which are executed in sequence by the worker.
Steps are never retried, only tasks.
It's just a queue and a state store — that's all you need.
Not every problem necessarily needs to scale to that level of complexity, and you can get quite far with much less.
key_insight: Durable workflows are simpler than industry tooling suggests, and for many cases Postgres alone can deliver reliable execution without extra services.
